scope.summary: Non-JS async runtime obligation attribution now covers Python event-loop drive, timeout, cancellation shield, thread-safe submission, thread offload helpers, and Swift continuation bridge functions.

capability_alignment.capability: map proof-backed non-JS async runtime bridge calls onto existing scheduling, cancellation, future-channel, callback, and exception obligations
not_a_feature_list: No new semantic kernel API was added.
No exact admission path was opened for Python asyncio helpers or Swift continuation bridges.
Python support reuses import-backed asyncio namespace and binding proof plus local module and same-file shadow guards.
Swift continuation support reuses the existing unshadowed Swift free-runtime-function guard used by task-group reporting.
The slice reuses future-drive, timer, task, future-settled, future-settlement, future-callback, cancellation/liveness, and exception-channel labels instead of adding selector-specific obligations.
reused_capabilities: Python ImportedNamespace and ImportedBinding symbol evidence
scope-aware Python alias and imported-binding shadow detection
Swift project-visible and same-file visible-name guards

new_reporting_surfaces.guard: The receiver must resolve to an import-backed asyncio namespace or from-import binding with no path-visible local asyncio module and no shadowing definition.
The receiver or binding must prove stdlib asyncio identity under the same alias/import and shadow guards.
The receiver or binding must prove stdlib asyncio identity under the same alias/import and shadow guards.
The receiver or binding must prove stdlib asyncio identity under the same alias/import and shadow guards.
The callee must be an unshadowed Swift free runtime function with no same-file or corpus-visible definition for the continuation symbol.

assertions: asyncio.run reports future-drive, future-settled, and exception obligations
asyncio.wait_for reports timer, cancellation/liveness, future-settled, and exception obligations
asyncio.shield reports cancellation/liveness and future-settled obligations
asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe and asyncio.to_thread report task and future-settled obligations
asyncio.to_thread also reports future callback demand/effect obligations
import asyncio as alias supports alias.run
from asyncio import wait_for as local supports local(...)
from asyncio import to_thread supports imported callback-offload attribution
Checked and unsafe continuation bridges report future-settled, settlement-continuation, and callback-demand/effect obligations
Throwing continuation variants also report exception-channel obligations
Non-throwing continuation variants do not report exception-channel obligations
Unimported asyncio.run does not report future-drive obligations
A shadowed asyncio alias does not report future-drive obligations
A shadowed from-import wait_for binding does not report timer obligations
A path-visible local asyncio.py blocks namespace and from-import helper attribution
A same-file continuation helper function blocks stdlib continuation attribution
A same-file continuation value binding blocks stdlib continuation attribution
A project-visible continuation helper function blocks stdlib continuation attribution

corpus_pricing_delta.reason: The pricing script now counts proof-backed Python event-loop/cancellation/thread helper surfaces and Swift continuation bridge functions as reporting-supported closed boundaries.

release_verify_timing.baseline_reference: Rust local self-field runtime provenance warm release verify real_seconds = 0.98
release_verify_timing.assessment: No large degradation observed; the warm run is close to the previous 0.98s reference and the implementation only adds small proof-gated string-label branches.

next_exact_work_policy.summary: These Python and Swift runtime bridge surfaces are now attributed precisely enough for recall-loss reporting, but exact recovery remains closed until scheduling, cancellation, result-channel, continuation resume, callback demand/effect, exception, and lifecycle contracts are dependency-closed.
